name: DRIVING THE USA
clue: It's the state with the most miles of Interstate Highway, more than 3,200; one Interstate accounts for 1/4 of that mileage

name: FOOD & DRINK HISTORY
clue: Its original name meant "bitter water" & it was made palatable to Europeans after the Spaniards added sugar

name: SINGERS
clue: He first recorded in 1939, "retired" in 1971, returned & had a Top 10 album in 1993

name: FASHION HISTORY
clue: In 1849 Americans traveling to the California gold fields via boat popularized this hat

name: 19th CENTURY NOVELS
clue: The author of this tale dedicated the novel to British philosopher William Godwin, her father

name: FOREIGN PHRASES
clue: Latin phrase found on the face of the Great Seal of the United States

name: CANADIAN CITIES
clue: The name of this provincial capital means "queen" in Latin

name: U.S. PRESIDENTS
clue: The 2 U.S. presidents who died at the youngest ages, 82 years apart
response: James A. Garfield & John F. Kennedy

name: DRAMA
clue: This play is divided into "parts", not acts, with the 2nd titled "The Gentleman Calls\"

name: EPITAPHS
clue: Her epitaph, from a 1925 poem by her, ends, "She knows that her dust is very pretty"; "dust" was in another she wrote for herself

name: WORD & PHRASE ORIGINS
clue: This term for a deadbeat came from a poker player whose hole card didn't fill out his hand

name: 19th CENTURY PERSONALITIES
clue: In 1876 this German wired the king of Greece, "I have gazed upon the face of Agamemnon\"

name: U.S. LANDMARKS
clue: In 1883 a Catholic diocese sold this to the state of Texas for $20,000

name: EXPLORERS
clue: Puerto Rico's 2nd largest city is named for this explorer, its 1st governor

name: WORLD CAPITALS
clue: Ruled at times by Persians, Mongols & Russians, Baku is the only capital that borders this body of water

name: CELEBRITY FATHERS & DAUGHTERS
clue: He won a Grammy in 1959, she won 3 in the 1970s & a song by both was 1991's Song & Record of the Year

name: FLAGS
clue: A flag bearing a white anchor & caduceus on a blue field represents this U.S. government official
response: Surgeon General of the United States

name: AMERICAN LITERATURE
clue: This first American writer to earn $1 million received only $2,000 for a 1903 novel set in the Klondike

name: CHEMICAL ELEMENTS
clue: Isolated in 1945 during uranium fission research, it was named for an ancient deity to suggest humans gaining a new power

name: THE GREEK ALPHABET
clue: Of the 24 letters of the Greek alphabet, it's the only one that ends in an English letter that none of the others does

name: FINAL RESTING PLACES
clue: A cemetery on this island has the graves of Robert Fulton & 2 of the first 4 Treasury Secretaries

name: THE NOBEL PRIZE
clue: It's the only Nobel category in which the prize has not been awarded to a woman

name: GEOGRAPHIC WORDPLAY
clue: The name of this Asian country contains the name of a Mediterranean volcano

name: SHOW BIZ MARRIAGES
clue: Married since 1977, the year of this band's 1st album, 2 members referenced another album with their 2023 "Remain in Love" tour

name: BOOK SERIES
clue: In the 8th book in a series by L. Frank Baum, this character begins to speak; he simply chose not to before

name: FOOD ETYMOLOGY
clue: Keith Downey developed rapeseed into this cooking product, now a huge cash crop for farmers in Saskatchewan

name: U.S. STRUCTURES
clue: On December 6, 1884 this was capped with a 100-oz., 9-inch-high pyramid-shaped block of aluminum, a metal that was rare at the time

name: BORN & DIED
clue: He was born in 1728 in Yorkshire, England & died in a skirmish February 14, 1779 in Kealakekua Bay, Hawaii

name: U.S. STATES
clue: It only has 3 communities with a population over 15,000 & is the least populous state east of the Mississippi

name: 19th CENTURY AUTHORS
clue: His works include "Sylvie and Bruno", "Phantasmagoria and Other Poems" & "Algebraic Formulae and Rules\"

name: RECENT FILMS
clue: One of its first lines is "I won't talk! I won't say a word!!!\"

name: AUTHORS
clue: On his death in 1862 a Massachusetts paper said, "No man ever lived closer to nature, and reported her secrets more eloquently\"

name: FAMOUS NAMES
clue: Surveyor general of India from 1830-43, in 1865 a mountain was renamed in his honor

name: POLITICAL CONNECTIONS
clue: In 1832 when the House tried Sam Houston for contempt, he was defended by this lawyer & lyricist

name: RULERS
clue: Last name of the last man to sit in power on the Peacock Throne

name: THE VOCAL ARTS
clue: Alessandro Moreschi, who died in 1922, was the last renowned member of this operatic category of voice

name: WORLD CAPITALS
clue: One of the 3 national capitals made up of 2 words that begin with the same letter
response: (1 of) Addis Ababa, San Salvador, or Phnom Penh

name: FICTIONAL WOMEN
clue: After dying, she's described as having "too much of water", & her brother says, "therefore I forbid my tears\"

name: GEOGRAPHIC NAMES
clue: In 1492 Columbus visited this island that he named for the country whose flag he flew

name: BRITISH HISTORY
clue: The 1st Tudor monarch of England, he was grandfather of the last Tudor monarch, Elizabeth I

name: BOTANY
clue: The genus of this herb used in crop rotation & to feed livestock is Trifolium

name: 8-LETTER WORDS
clue: Differing by one letter, the 2 words that mean job & time away from the job

name: U.S. LANDMARKS
clue: This building has the world's biggest switchboard with about 1 million calls per day on 34,500 lines

name: PRESIDENTS
clue: Of the 5 vowels, only these are the 1st letter of a president's last name

name: PULITZER PRIZE-WINNING NOVELS
clue: Its first line is "A green hunting cap squeezed on the top of the fleshy balloon of a head\"

name: THE HISTORY OF FLIGHT
clue: In 1784 these 2 future presidents saw an early manned balloon flight in Paris &, in 1793, America's 1st, in Philadelphia

name: WORLD MAGAZINES
clue: The name of this German newsweekly means "The Mirror\"

name: THE VATICAN
clue: A statue of this man is being erected inside the Vatican's walls near where he was locked up in 1633

name: CONSUMER PRODUCTS
clue: This product was reintroduced in 1906 with trimethylxanthine as the sole remaining stimulant

name: WORLD CITIES
clue: The northernmost city with a population over 5 million, it was founded in 1703 & its name was changed 3 times in the 20th century

name: WINTER OLYMPIC SPORTS
clue: The official Olympic website says this event "has its roots in survival skills" practiced in the snowy forests of Scandinavia

name: FAMOUS AMERICANS
clue: Among the 10 modern martyrs who will have statues in Westminster Abbey is this American, 1929-1968

name: FAMOUS RELATIVES
clue: Around 1780 this grandfather of Charles Darwin invented a pyrometer to measure high heat in his kilns

name: U.S. CONGRESS
clue: This state has the largest congressional district in area

name: DANTE'S INFERNO
clue: During the journey, Dante encounters Homer, Socrates & Cicero, who bide their time in the first circle, aka this

name: MODERN TECHNOLOGY
clue: Common name given Douglas Engelbart's device, an "X-Y position indicator for a display system\"

name: NYC TV
clue: In 2010, in its fourth season, this TV show shifted its primary setting to 6th Avenue, 2 blocks west

name: ARTISTS
clue: \"Red Vineyard" was one of the few of his paintings sold during his lifetime

name: FEMALE AUTHORS
clue: At age 9 in 1883 she moved west, where she met Annie Pavelka, a young pioneer on whom she would later model a title character

name: LEADING MEN OF THE MOVIES
clue: This actor who turned down the role of Dirty Harry played 142 leading roles, a Guinness record

name: CHEMICAL NAMES
clue: The name of this pungent gaseous compound is ultimately derived from the top god of the ancient Egyptians

name: AFRICAN GEOGRAPHY
clue: After the secession of South Sudan from Sudan in 2011, this became the largest country in Africa by area

name: IN THE ENCYCLOPEDIA BRITANNICA
clue: The last full 2005 Micropedia article about a person is on the Russian-born man famed as an inventor of this in the 1920s

name: 21st CENTURY BROADWAY
clue: A review of this musical noted "sacrilege" & said it was "blasphemous" but "its heart is as pure as... a Rodgers & Hammerstein show\"

name: INTERNATIONAL BEVERAGE BRANDS
clue: The name of this popular beer brand founded in 1897 is a reference to the 20th century

name: 19th CENTURY POEMS
clue: Written about the U.S. occupation of the Philippines, a Kipling poem said, "Take up" this now-controversial phrase

name: 20th CENTURY FICTION
clue: The author's foreword to this novel says, "When I read it now I feel myself back again on the steamer from Aswan to Wadi Halfa\"

name: WOMEN OF HISTORY
clue: Regarding the idea of "women first", she queried, "Women demand equal rights on land—why not on sea?\"

name: ARMIES OF THE WORLD
clue: The national orchestra of this nation on the Mediterranean Sea is larger than its army

name: MUSIC LEGENDS
clue: These 2 "monarchs" of popular culture both passed away on August 16, one in 2018, the other 41 years earlier

name: SEE THE USA
clue: Latest figures show this U.S. state is the most popular destination for Japanese tourists

name: AMERICAN WRITERS
clue: In 1936 the San Francisco News sent this man to investigate living conditions among migrant workers

name: SPORTS
clue: They are the only Major League Baseball team which prints their media guide in 2 languages

name: LITERARY QUOTES
clue: \"I would like to take the great DiMaggio fishing" is a line from this 1952 work; like DiMaggio, it's an American classic
response: The Old Man and the Sea (by Ernest Hemingway)

name: POETRY
clue: In a poem about this battle, Robert Browning wrote, "To Akropolis! Run, Pheidippides, one race more\"

name: FAMOUS WEDDINGS
clue: In 1998 a 61-year-old piece of this couple's wedding cake sold for $26,000 at Sotheby's
response: The Duke of Windsor (former King Edward VIII) & Wallis Simpson

name: FAMOUS TEACHERS
clue: In 1967 this former teacher published a memoir entitled "Center of the Storm\"

name: U.S. POLITICS
clue: Since 1960, only Massachusetts & this state have produced more than one of the 10 Democratic presidential nominees

name: AUTHORS
clue: In 1890 he witnessed a mild cyclone in Aberdeen, South Dakota, fodder for his most famous novel

name: MEN OF PARIS
clue: When the body of this man who died in 1870 was moved in 2002, a cloth with the motto "Un pour tous, tous pour un" was used

name: THE WORLD AFTER WWII
clue: This peninsula was divided when Japan surrendered to the U.S. below the 38th parallel & to the Soviet Union north of it

name: FAMOUS WOMEN
clue: During WWI this American showed off her talents in a play called "The Western Girl\"

name: U.S. TERRITORIES
clue: 5 of the 7 islands that make up this U.S. territory are of volcanic origin; the other 2 are coral atolls

name: WORLD CAPITALS
clue: This city called "The Mother of Russian Cities" is capital of a country other than Russia

name: SUPREME COURT JUSTICES
clue: He served as assistant attorney general of Missouri under John C. Danforth from 1974 to 1977

name: 20th CENTURY PHYSICS
clue: Puzzlingly heavy & long-lived particles discovered in the 1940s were dubbed this adjective later applied to even smaller particles

name: CLASSIC CHILDREN'S LITERATURE
clue: A 2016 biography of a children's author is titled "In the Great Green Room", a line from this classic book

name: GEOGRAPHIC PAIRS
clue: By ferry, the distance between these 2 paired Mediterranean islands is about 40 miles from Alcudia to Ciutadella

name: ACADEMY AWARD NOMINATIONS
clue: He was nominated for Best Actor 5 times in the 1950s, the most for any man in one decade

name: TRANSPORTATION USA
clue: This public agency runs the USA's busiest bus terminal, opened in 1950 for commuters awed by its polished steel & stone
response: the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey

name: 21st CENTURY OSCARS
clue: Before she was 25, she became the youngest performer to receive a second Best Actress nomination

name: THE NATIONAL STATUARY HALL COLLECTION
clue: In 2009 Alabama replaced a statue in the collection with one of her at age 7, the first child honored

name: U.S. TERRITORIES
clue: Of the 5 inhabited U.S. territories, this is the only one where cars drive on the left

name: CHILDREN'S AUTHORS
clue: \"The Tale of Kitty-in-Boots", written by her in 1914, was first published in 2016

name: SHAKESPEAREAN HEROINES
clue: The name of this heroine known for her filial devotion is probably derived from the Latin for "heart\"

name: MILITARY HISTORY
clue: A 1918 article titled "Do Not Shoot at" these said hunters were interfering with the U.S. Signal Corps' training of them

name: POP CULTURE
clue: Also the title of one of the best-selling albums of all time, it was first seen in Russian photos taken in 1959
response: the dark side of the Moon

name: THE MOVIES
clue: Later a Broadway show, this 1988 comedy was the first movie directed by a woman to earn $100 million

name: EPITAPHS
clue: From his own 1898 poem, the epitaph of this Irish-born man ends, "his mourners will be outcast men, & outcasts always mourn\"

name: AWARDS
clue: The Maggie Awards given by the Planned Parenthood Federation of America are named for her
